officialSourceLabel: IRS Topic No. 409 — Capital Gains and Losses

longTerm.note: Applies to assets held more than 12 months. Thresholds based on taxable income (after deductions). Source: IRS Rev. Proc. 2025-32.

shortTerm.note: Assets held 12 months or less. Taxed as ordinary income at standard federal income tax rates (10%–37%).

niit.note: Net Investment Income Tax (3.8%) applies when MAGI exceeds threshold. NOT inflation-adjusted since 2013.

states.name: Alabama
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 5%.

states.name: Alaska
states.note: No state income tax. No capital gains tax.

states.name: Arizona
states.note: Flat 2.5% income tax rate including capital gains as of 2023. One of the lowest in the nation.

states.name: Arkansas
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 3.9% as of 2024.

states.name: California
states.note: Highest state capital gains tax in the US. No preferential treatment for long-term gains — all gains taxed as ordinary income. 1% mental health surcharge applies above $1M income, bringing effective top to 14.4%.

states.name: Colorado
states.note: Flat 4.4% state income tax rate including capital gains.

states.name: Connecticut
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top marginal rate 6.99%.

states.name: Delaware
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 6.6%.

states.name: Florida
states.note: No state income tax. No capital gains tax.

states.name: Georgia
states.note: Flat 5.49% state income tax including capital gains as of 2024, reducing to 4.99% by 2029.

states.name: Hawaii
states.note: Long-term capital gains taxed at lower preferential rate. Top rate 7.25% on long-term gains. Ordinary income top rate is 11%.

states.name: Idaho
states.note: Flat 5.8% state income tax including capital gains.

states.name: Illinois
states.note: Flat 4.95% state income tax including capital gains.

states.name: Indiana
states.note: Flat 3.05% state income tax including capital gains.

states.name: Iowa
states.note: Flat 5.75% state income tax including capital gains as of 2024. Iowa is phasing down to 3.9% flat rate by 2026 under 2022 tax reform.

states.name: Kansas
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 5.7%.

states.name: Kentucky
states.note: Flat 4.0% state income tax including capital gains.

states.name: Louisiana
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Louisiana reduced top income tax rate to 3% effective January 1, 2025.

states.name: Maine
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 7.15% on income over $70,350 (single).

states.name: Maryland
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. State top rate 5.75% plus local income taxes of 2.25%–3.2% depending on county.

states.name: Massachusetts
states.note: Most capital gains taxed at flat 5% income tax rate. Short-term capital gains taxed at 8.5% for assets held less than 1 year. Gains on certain assets held 1–5 years have different treatment.

states.name: Michigan
states.note: Flat 4.25% state income tax including capital gains.

states.name: Minnesota
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 9.85% — one of the highest in the nation.

states.name: Mississippi
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 4.7% as of 2024, scheduled to reduce further.

states.name: Missouri
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 4.8% as of 2024.

states.name: Montana
states.note: Montana offers a capital gains credit — effective rate on long-term gains is approximately 4.5%. Top ordinary income rate is higher.

states.name: Nebraska
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 5.84%.

states.name: Nevada
states.note: No state income tax. No capital gains tax.

states.name: New Hampshire
states.note: No state income tax and no capital gains tax. New Hampshire repealed its Interest and Dividends Tax effective January 1, 2025.

states.name: New Jersey
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 10.75% — one of the highest in the nation.

states.name: New Mexico
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 5.9%.

states.name: New York
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. State top rate 10.9%. NYC residents add 3.876% city income tax, bringing combined state+city rate to approximately 14.776% — among the highest in the country.

states.name: North Carolina
states.note: Flat 4.5% state income tax including capital gains as of 2024, with further reductions planned.

states.name: North Dakota
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 2.5% — one of the lowest income tax states.

states.name: Ohio
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 3.5% as of 2023. Ohio has no separate capital gains tax.

states.name: Oklahoma
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 4.75%.

states.name: Oregon
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 9.9% — one of the highest in the nation. Oregon also has no sales tax, so capital gains tax is a key state revenue source.

states.name: Pennsylvania
states.note: Flat 3.07% state income tax including capital gains.

states.name: Rhode Island
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 5.99%.

states.name: South Carolina
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 6.4%. South Carolina offers a 44% deduction on long-term capital gains, effectively reducing the rate to approximately 3.6% for long-term gains.

states.name: South Dakota
states.note: No state income tax. No capital gains tax.

states.name: Tennessee
states.note: No state income tax on wages or capital gains. Hall Income Tax was fully repealed in 2021.

states.name: Texas
states.note: No state income tax. No capital gains tax.

states.name: Utah
states.note: Flat 4.55% state income tax including capital gains.

states.name: Vermont
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 8.75%.

states.name: Virginia
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 5.75%.

states.name: Washington
states.note: Washington has no general income tax but levies a 7% capital gains tax on long-term gains above $262,000 per year (inflation-adjusted). Primary residence sales and charitable donations are excluded. Survived state Supreme Court challenge in 2023.

states.name: West Virginia
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. Top rate 6.5%.

states.name: Wisconsin
states.note: Long-term capital gains from most assets qualify for a 30% exclusion, effectively reducing the top rate from 7.65% to approximately 5.355%. Some assets have different treatment.

states.name: Wyoming
states.note: No state income tax. No capital gains tax.

states.name: Washington D.C.
states.note: Capital gains taxed as ordinary income. DC top income tax rate 10.75%.

faqs.question: What are the 2026 long-term capital gains tax rates?
faqs.answer: For 2026, the federal long-term capital gains tax rates are 0%, 15%, and 20%, depending on your taxable income and filing status. Single filers pay 0% on income up to $49,450, 15% from $49,451 to $545,500, and 20% above $545,500. Married filing jointly pays 0% up to $98,900 and 20% above $613,700. These rates apply to assets held more than 12 months.

faqs.question: What is the difference between short-term and long-term capital gains?
faqs.answer: Short-term capital gains apply to assets held 12 months or less and are taxed at ordinary income tax rates (10%–37%). Long-term capital gains apply to assets held more than 12 months and receive preferential federal rates of 0%, 15%, or 20%. The one-year holding threshold is one of the most important distinctions in US tax law.

faqs.question: What is the Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT)?
faqs.answer: The NIIT is a 3.8% federal surtax on net investment income — including capital gains, dividends, and interest — for taxpayers whose Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI) exceeds $200,000 for single filers or $250,000 for married filing jointly. These thresholds have not been adjusted for inflation since 2013, meaning more taxpayers are subject to the NIIT each year.

faqs.question: Which states have no capital gains tax?
faqs.answer: Nine states have no state-level capital gains tax: Alaska, Florida, Nevada, New Hampshire, South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Washington (for gains under $262,000), and Wyoming. Note that New Hampshire repealed its Interest and Dividends Tax effective January 1, 2025, making it fully tax-free on capital gains.

faqs.question: Which state has the highest capital gains tax?
faqs.answer: California has the highest state capital gains tax rate at 13.3% (14.4% with the 1% mental health surcharge on income over $1 million). California treats all capital gains as ordinary income with no preferential treatment for long-term gains. For high earners, combined federal and California tax on long-term gains can exceed 37%.

faqs.question: Do I pay capital gains tax when I sell my home?
faqs.answer: Usually not fully. The IRS allows a home sale exclusion of up to $250,000 in gains for single filers and up to $500,000 for married couples filing jointly, provided you owned and lived in the home as your primary residence for at least 2 of the last 5 years. Gains above these thresholds are taxable as long-term capital gains if you held the home over a year.

faqs.question: What is the maximum combined federal and state capital gains tax rate?
faqs.answer: For California residents, the combined maximum rate is approximately 37.1% on long-term gains for the highest earners — the 20% federal rate plus 3.8% NIIT plus 13.3% California state rate. With California's 1% mental health surcharge for income over $1M, the effective combined top rate reaches approximately 38.1%. New York City residents face combined rates of approximately 38.5% when factoring in the 3.876% NYC city income tax.

faqs.question: Are capital gains taxed differently for real estate vs stocks?
faqs.answer: The basic long-term 0%/15%/20% federal rates apply to both. However, real estate has a special rule: the unrecaptured Section 1250 gain (depreciation recapture) is taxed at a maximum 25% federal rate, higher than the standard 20% maximum. Primary home sales also qualify for the $250,000/$500,000 exclusion. Qualified Opportunity Zone investments offer deferral and potential exclusion benefits.
